settings.title: Three Ways to Make Cut Flowers Last Twice as Long
settings.excerpt: Skip the little packet of powder and do these instead, and your stems will thank you.
settings.body:
Customers often tell us their flowers wilt within days. Usually a few small habits are all that stand between a sad vase and a thriving one.

Recut the stems at an angle. Do it under running water every couple of days so they can keep drinking.

Change the water often. Cloudy water is full of bacteria. Fresh water every two days does more than any flower food.

Keep them cool. Away from direct sun and ripening fruit, which gives off a gas that ages blooms fast.

Why these beat the little packet

That sachet of powder is mostly sugar and a touch of bleach, which helps a little but does not solve the real problem. Stems wilt because the channels they drink through get blocked by bacteria, and because the cut end seals over. Recutting reopens the stem, and clean water keeps the bacteria from building up in the first place. Do both regularly and you are treating the cause rather than masking it, which is why they work better than anything you stir into the vase.

The fruit bowl catches people out more than anything else. Ripening fruit gives off a gas that tells flowers to age, so a vase next to the bananas fades fast for reasons that have nothing to do with the flowers themselves.

A simple weekly rhythm

  • Recut the stems and change the water every two days.
  • Pull any leaves that have slipped below the waterline.
  • Move the vase off a sunny sill and away from the fruit bowl.

None of this takes more than a minute or two, and it routinely doubles how long a bunch lasts. Skip the powder if you like. These habits do more.
